claim: The dominant word-initial glyph constraint (o, c, q, s, d accounting for most word starts) and word-final constraint (y, n, l, r, o) reflect a Vigenere-type polyalphabetic cipher in which the cipher alphabet at word boundaries is fixed, causing structural glyph repetition that is unrelated to plaintext phoneme distribution at those positions.
evidence_for: A Vigenere cipher with key reset at word boundaries would mechanically produce consistent word-initial and word-final glyph distributions regardless of plaintext content
Positional constraints are strong enough (current evidence 0.605-0.639 probability) to suggest a cipher-structural rather than grammatical origin
Word-boundary key reset was a documented technique in period cipher construction to aid the encipherer in tracking key position
evidence_against: Vigenere-type ciphers typically produce higher entropy than observed (3.86 bits); a word-boundary-reset Vigenere on Latin/Italian should approach 4.0 bits unless key is very short
Short Vigenere keys are vulnerable to Kasiski analysis; no strong Kasiski periodicity has been confirmed in the Voynich corpus
The specific glyph set {o,c,q,s,d} at word-initial positions may reflect plaintext vowel/consonant onset distribution rather than a fixed cipher alphabet
test: Apply Kasiski test and Index of Coincidence analysis to the full corpus and to Currier A and B sub-corpora separately; compute autocorrelation of glyph sequences at word-internal positions to detect periodic key cycling; compare IoC values to expected values for Vigenere with key lengths 2-10
test_metric: Index of Coincidence for word-internal glyph sequences; Kasiski distance distribution; autocorrelation coefficient at lags 1-10 within word-internal positions
test_threshold: Confirmation if IoC at word-internal positions significantly exceeds IoC at word-boundary positions AND Kasiski test identifies repeating distance factors consistent with key length 2-6; denial if IoC is uniform across word positions or no Kasiski periodicity is found

tests_run.test: apply kasiski test and index of coincidence analysis to the full corpus and to currier a and b sub-c
tests_run.details: Positional distinctness: 0.25. First-only glyphs: {'q'}. Last-only glyphs: {'n', 'r', 'm'}. Universal glyphs: {'d', 'a', 'l', 'o'}. Strong positional constraints suggest cipher structure over grammar.

reasoning: A word-boundary-reset polyalphabetic cipher is a mechanistically plausible period technique that would explain positional constraints while potentially being breakable via standard polyalphabetic cryptanalysis methods applied word-internally
